required.title: [Book] Structures: Or Why Things Don't Fall Down
required.author: J.E. Gordon

required.title: [Materials] Masonry tools (trowels, levels, string lines, jointing tools), mortar and concrete materials, rebar (#4 and #5), form lumber, gravel for drainage, landscape fabric
[Tool] 4-foot level, plumb bob, transit or laser level for foundation layout

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Essential Craftsman — retaining wall and foundation videos

supplementary.title: [Reference] Local building codes for masonry construction

competence_markers.description: The student can design and build a reinforced masonry retaining wall with proper drainage that resists actual lateral earth pressure
The student can trace load paths through a masonry structure and identify the weakest points
The student can diagnose masonry failures (settlement, overload, water damage, thermal movement) from visible evidence
The student can perform tuckpointing repairs with proper mortar matching and joint profile
The student can analyze a historical masonry structure and explain the engineering principles that make it work
The student can teach masonry skills to a less experienced student effectively and safely
The student understands the boundary between work they can perform and work that requires engineering review or permits

content.why_this_matters:
You have laid brick, mixed concrete, built arches, and experienced compression physics firsthand. Now you step into the domain of structural masonry — work that bears real load, holds back real earth, supports real buildings, and must be right because the consequences of getting it wrong are not cosmetic but catastrophic.

Structural masonry is where the physics you learned in Weathering Storms becomes engineering. A retaining wall that holds back a hillside is not a garden wall scaled up — it is a fundamentally different problem involving lateral earth pressure, drainage, overturning moments, and sliding resistance. A foundation wall that supports a building above it must distribute load to the footing below without cracking, settling unevenly, or allowing water infiltration. These are problems that require analysis, not just technique.

You will also learn to read failure. Every crack in a masonry wall tells a story — about settling, overloading, water damage, poor original construction, or thermal movement. The person who can look at a cracked wall and diagnose what went wrong has a diagnostic mind that transfers to every complex system. And the person who can then design and execute a repair — tuckpointing deteriorated joints, rebuilding a failed section, underpinning a settling foundation — has a capability that is genuinely rare and genuinely valuable.

Finally, you will connect your hands-on experience to the full arc of human construction. The Roman aqueducts, the Gothic cathedrals, the Inca dry-stone walls that survive earthquakes — these are not mysteries when you understand compression, load paths, and material behavior. You will see ancient builders not as inexplicable geniuses but as practitioners of the same principles you have learned, applied with extraordinary skill and patience over decades and centuries.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Structural masonry design** — the ability to design masonry structures that bear significant load: retaining walls, foundation walls, columns, and piers
2. **Load distribution analysis** — tracing forces through masonry structures from point of application to foundation, identifying weak points
3. **Retaining wall engineering** — understanding lateral earth pressure, overturning resistance, drainage, and the specific failure modes of retaining structures
4. **Failure diagnosis** — reading cracks, settlements, and deterioration to identify the cause and severity of masonry failures
5. **Repair and restoration** — tuckpointing, crack repair, section replacement, and underpinning as practical skills
6. **Historical construction analysis** — understanding the engineering principles behind historical masonry structures
7. **Teaching masonry** — the ability to instruct less experienced students in masonry techniques and principles
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Structural Masonry — Beyond the Garden Wall

A garden wall holds itself up. A structural masonry wall holds other things up — or holds other things back. This distinction changes everything about how you design, build, and evaluate the work.

**Load-bearing walls** carry the weight of the structure above them — roof, floors, and everything on those floors — and transfer it downward through the wall to the foundation and into the ground. Every brick, every mortar joint, every course in a load-bearing wall is part of a load path. If any point in that path is weaker than the load demands, the wall fails at that point.

**Retaining walls** resist lateral force — the horizontal pressure of soil pushing against them. This is a fundamentally different problem than carrying vertical load. A retaining wall must resist overturning (the soil pushing the wall over), sliding (the soil pushing the wall horizontally), and bearing failure (the wall pushing the soil beneath its foundation beyond its capacity).

**Columns and piers** carry concentrated loads — the weight of a beam resting on a single point. The column must transfer that concentrated load to the foundation without crushing. Column failure in masonry is typically by buckling (the column bows outward under load) or by crushing (the compressive stress exceeds the material capacity).

**The fundamental principle:** Every masonry structure must keep its materials in compression. When tension is unavoidable, reinforcement must handle it. When lateral forces are present, the structure must resist them through geometry (width, batter, buttresses), through its own weight, or through reinforcement. Understanding which forces are present and how the structure resists them is the core skill of structural masonry.

### Part 2: Load Distribution Through Masonry

Forces do not simply pass straight down through a masonry wall. They spread.

**The 45-degree rule:** A concentrated load (a beam bearing on a wall, for example) spreads at approximately 45 degrees through solid masonry. If a beam delivers a 2,000-pound load to a 4-inch-wide bearing plate on top of a wall, that load has spread to approximately 12 inches of wall width by the time it has traveled 8 inches down — 4 inches of bearing plate plus 4 inches of spread on each side. By the time it reaches the foundation, it may have spread across several feet of wall, depending on the wall height.

**Why this matters:** Load distribution explains why masonry walls can carry enormous loads — the weight spreads out as it travels down, so the stress at the base is much lower than the stress at the point of application. It also explains why openings in walls are critical — a door or window interrupts the load path, and the load must be redirected around the opening.

**Lintels and arches over openings:** When you cut a hole in a load-bearing wall for a door or window, you interrupt the load path. The weight above the opening must be carried across the void and delivered to the wall sections on either side. A lintel (a horizontal beam, typically steel or reinforced concrete) spans the opening and carries the load. An arch redirects the load through compression along its curved form. Without a lintel or arch, the masonry above the opening has no support, and it will crack and eventually collapse.

**Reading the evidence:** A diagonal crack running upward from the corner of a window tells you that the lintel is inadequate — the load is trying to find its way around the opening and the masonry is cracking along the path of maximum stress. A vertical crack above the center of a window tells you that the lintel is deflecting — bending under load and pulling the masonry apart at the top. These cracks are not random. They are the structure telling you exactly what is wrong.

### Part 3: Retaining Walls — Holding Back the Earth

Retaining walls are among the most common masonry structures in residential construction, and retaining wall failure is among the most common structural failures. Understanding why they fail is understanding how to build them correctly.

**Lateral earth pressure:** Soil pushes against a retaining wall with a force that increases with depth. At the surface, the pressure is nearly zero. At the base of the wall, the pressure is at its maximum. This is because the weight of the soil above any given point creates the horizontal pressure at that point — more soil above means more pressure. The total force on the wall is proportional to the square of the wall height — double the height and you quadruple the total force. This is why retaining wall design becomes dramatically more critical as height increases.

**Failure modes:**

*Overturning:* The soil pushes the wall, and the wall tips forward, rotating around its toe (the front bottom edge). The wall resists overturning through its own weight — a heavier wall is harder to tip. The resistance is also affected by the wall's width: a wider base creates a longer lever arm for the wall's weight to resist the overturning moment. This is why retaining walls are typically thicker at the base than at the top (battered) or have a wide footing that extends behind the wall.

*Sliding:* The soil pushes the wall horizontally, and the wall slides forward on its foundation. The wall resists sliding through friction between the footing and the soil beneath it, and through passive earth pressure against the front of the footing (soil in front of the footing resists horizontal movement). A key (a projecting ridge on the bottom of the footing that digs into the soil below) dramatically increases sliding resistance.

*Bearing failure:* The combination of vertical load (the wall's own weight) and the overturning moment creates uneven pressure on the soil beneath the footing — higher pressure at the toe, lower pressure at the heel. If the toe pressure exceeds the soil's bearing capacity, the soil fails and the wall settles forward.

**Drainage:** The most common cause of retaining wall failure is water. Water behind a retaining wall dramatically increases the lateral pressure — saturated soil is heavier than dry soil, and water itself exerts hydrostatic pressure. Proper drainage — gravel backfill behind the wall, perforated drain pipe at the base, weep holes through the wall — is not optional. It is the single most important element of retaining wall design. A well-designed retaining wall with poor drainage will eventually fail. A modestly designed retaining wall with excellent drainage will likely stand for decades.

**Reinforcement in retaining walls:** Masonry retaining walls above 3-4 feet typically require vertical reinforcement (rebar in grouted cores) to resist the bending forces created by lateral earth pressure. The reinforcement goes on the tension side — the back face of the wall (the side facing the retained soil), because that face is being pulled apart as the soil pushes the wall forward. Horizontal reinforcement (bond beam blocks with rebar) provides additional lateral strength and ties the wall together.

### Part 4: Diagnosing Masonry Failures

Every crack in a masonry structure tells a story. Learning to read that story is one of the most valuable skills in construction.

**Settlement cracks:** When the ground beneath a foundation settles unevenly, the structure above it cracks. Settlement cracks are typically diagonal, starting at the point of maximum differential movement. A building that settles more on the left side than the right will show diagonal cracks that step downward toward the left — the cracks follow the stair-step pattern of the mortar joints because the mortar joints are the weakest plane in the wall.

**Overload cracks:** When a masonry wall carries more load than it was designed for, it cracks vertically. The compressive force pushes the wall material outward (Poisson's effect), and the mortar joints, which are weaker than the masonry units, crack first. Vertical cracks in a load-bearing wall, especially if they are widening over time, are a serious structural concern.

**Thermal movement cracks:** Masonry expands when heated and contracts when cooled. Long walls without expansion joints develop cracks as the masonry tries to accommodate thermal movement. These cracks are typically vertical and appear at regular intervals or at points of stress concentration (near openings, at corners, at changes in wall thickness).

**Water damage and frost:** Water that enters masonry through cracks, failed mortar joints, or capillary action causes progressive damage. In freeze-thaw climates, water in the masonry expands when it freezes, cracking the mortar and eventually the masonry units themselves. This damage is called spalling — the surface of the brick or stone flakes away, exposing fresh material to more water, which causes more spalling. The cycle is self-accelerating.

**Efflorescence:** White powdery deposits on masonry surfaces. Water migrating through the wall dissolves salts in the mortar or masonry units and deposits them on the surface as it evaporates. Efflorescence itself is mostly cosmetic, but it is evidence that water is moving through the wall — and that water movement may be causing invisible damage behind the surface.

**The diagnostic method:** When you approach a failed masonry structure, you observe systematically. Where are the cracks? What pattern do they follow? Are they active (still widening) or stable? What is the age and history of the structure? What loads does it carry? What is the soil condition? What is the water management situation? Each piece of evidence narrows the diagnosis. The goal is not to guess but to reason from evidence to cause.

### Part 5: Repair and Restoration

Masonry repair is often harder than new construction because you must work within the constraints of what already exists.

**Tuckpointing (repointing):** The most common masonry repair. Mortar joints deteriorate over time due to weather, freeze-thaw cycles, and water infiltration. Tuckpointing removes the deteriorated mortar to a depth of at least 3/4 inch (use a grinder with a diamond blade or a cold chisel — never damage the masonry units) and replaces it with fresh mortar matched to the original in color, composition, and joint profile.

The mortar match matters. Modern Portland cement mortar is significantly harder than historic lime mortar. If you repoint a historic brick wall with Portland cement mortar, the mortar will be harder than the bricks. When the wall moves (thermal expansion, settlement, vibration), the stress concentrates in the bricks instead of the mortar joints, and the bricks crack and spall. Historic masonry must be repointed with mortar of similar or softer composition than the original. This is one of the most common and most destructive mistakes in masonry repair.

**Crack repair:** Small cracks in mortar joints are repaired by tuckpointing. Cracks through masonry units (bricks or stones) indicate structural movement and require assessment of the underlying cause before repair. Patching a crack caused by ongoing settlement without addressing the settlement is treating a symptom, not a disease — the crack will return.

**Section replacement:** When a section of masonry has failed beyond repair (extensive spalling, crushed units, structural displacement), the failed section is removed and rebuilt. This requires shoring the structure above (temporarily supporting the load while the section below is removed), removing the failed material, rebuilding with matching materials and techniques, and carefully integrating the new work with the existing structure.

**Underpinning:** When a foundation has settled or is inadequate for its load, underpinning extends or strengthens the foundation. Traditional underpinning involves excavating beneath the existing footing in small sections, pouring new concrete to a greater depth, and allowing each section to cure before excavating the next. This is precision work with significant risk — you are excavating beneath a loaded structure.

### Part 6: Historical Construction Analysis

With your hands-on experience in masonry, you are now equipped to understand some of humanity's greatest engineering achievements.

**Roman concrete (opus caementicium):** The Romans developed a concrete using volcanic ash (pozzolana) that reacted with lime and seawater to form a material that actually got stronger over time. Roman marine concrete structures are more durable than modern Portland cement concrete in marine environments. The Pantheon's dome — 142 feet in diameter, unreinforced concrete — has stood for nearly 1,900 years. The Romans understood that they could reduce the weight of the dome by using progressively lighter aggregate toward the top (heavy basalt at the base, light pumice at the crown) and by incorporating coffers (recessed panels) that reduced mass without compromising the shell structure.

**Gothic pointed arches and flying buttresses:** The Gothic builders solved a problem that had limited Romanesque architecture. A round arch generates outward thrust that must be resisted by massive walls. The pointed arch, because it is steeper, generates less outward thrust for the same span. This allowed thinner walls. But thinner walls still needed to resist the remaining thrust — so the Gothic builders invented the flying buttress, an external arched support that carries the thrust from the vault across an open space to a heavy pier. This system — pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and flying buttresses — allowed the enormous window openings and soaring interiors that define Gothic cathedrals.

**Inca dry-stone construction:** The Inca built without mortar, using stones cut so precisely that a knife blade cannot be inserted between them. The stones were shaped with harder stones (no metal tools) and fitted together with interlocking irregular faces. These walls flex during earthquakes rather than cracking — the stones shift slightly and then settle back into place. This is seismic engineering achieved through geometry and craftsmanship, not reinforcement.

**What connects all of these:** Compression. Every one of these structures keeps its materials in compression. The Roman dome is in compression throughout. The Gothic vault transmits its weight through compression in the ribs and arches to the buttresses to the piers to the ground. The Inca walls rely on gravity and friction (both compression phenomena) to hold their stones in place. The builders understood — through experience, intuition, and generations of accumulated knowledge — the same principles you have learned through your own hands-on work.

### Part 7: Teaching Masonry

The capstone test of your understanding is the ability to teach it.

Teaching masonry to a less experienced student requires you to break complex skills into learnable steps, to demonstrate technique with precision, to identify errors before they compound, and to explain the physics behind the technique so the student understands not just what to do but why.

When you teach someone to lay brick, you must be able to explain mortar consistency, demonstrate proper trowel technique, show what level and plumb look like and why they matter, correct joint width without discouraging the student, and build the student's rhythm gradually — mortar, brick, check.

When you teach concrete work, you must explain hydration chemistry in terms the student can understand, demonstrate why water ratio matters, show proper placement and consolidation, and supervise curing practices.

Teaching is where you discover the gaps in your own understanding. If you cannot explain why a retaining wall needs drainage, you do not fully understand it yourself. If you cannot demonstrate proper tuckpointing technique, your technique needs refinement. The student you are teaching is also teaching you — by asking questions you have not considered and making mistakes you have not anticipated.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Structural Design and Construction
**Developing well:** The student designs a retaining wall or structural element with appropriate dimensions, reinforcement, and drainage. The design accounts for actual site conditions. Construction matches the design. The finished structure is plumb, level, properly reinforced, and properly drained. The student can explain every design decision and why it matters.

**Developing slowly:** The student copies a design from a book without adapting it to site conditions. The construction deviates from the design without understanding why that matters. Reinforcement is placed but without understanding of where tension occurs. Drainage is included but not properly executed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student understand lateral earth pressure conceptually, or are they just following a formula? Can they explain what would happen if the drainage failed? Can they identify which part of their structure is most likely to fail first and why?

### 2. Failure Diagnosis
**Developing well:** The student examines a cracked or deteriorated masonry structure and produces a coherent diagnosis: the type of failure (settlement, overload, water, thermal), the likely cause, whether it is active or stable, and what repair is appropriate. The diagnosis is supported by specific evidence — crack patterns, location, width, and orientation.

**Developing slowly:** The student sees cracks but cannot distinguish types or causes. They guess rather than reason from evidence. They cannot explain why a crack runs in a particular direction or what the pattern indicates.

**Diagnostic questions:** Give the student photographs of different masonry failures and ask them to diagnose each one. Can they distinguish settlement from overload? Can they identify water damage versus thermal cracking? Can they explain their reasoning?

### 3. Repair Skills
**Developing well:** Tuckpointing is clean, consistent, and properly matched to the original mortar. The student understands when mortar matching matters (always, but especially on historic structures) and can explain why. Crack repairs address the underlying cause, not just the visible symptom.

**Developing slowly:** Tuckpointing is messy, inconsistent in depth or profile, or uses inappropriate mortar. The student patches cracks without considering the underlying cause.

**Diagnostic questions:** Show the student a poorly repointed wall (modern hard mortar on soft historic brick) and ask them to identify what was done wrong and what the consequences will be. Can they explain the mortar hardness relationship?

### 4. Historical Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can look at a historical masonry structure and explain the engineering — not just describe what they see but analyze how forces flow, why the form works, and what principles the original builders were applying. They see ancient builders as skilled practitioners, not as mysteries.

**Developing slowly:** The student can describe historical structures but cannot analyze them. They know that Roman arches are strong but cannot explain why in terms of compression and load paths.

**Diagnostic questions:** Show the student an image of a flying buttress and ask them to trace the load path from vault to ground. Can they explain why the buttress is shaped the way it is? Can they explain what would happen if it were removed?

### 5. Teaching Effectiveness
**Developing well:** The student plans a lesson appropriate for the learner's level, demonstrates clearly, catches and corrects errors constructively, maintains safety, and produces a learner who can complete the task with reasonable quality. The student adapts their teaching when the learner struggles.

**Developing slowly:** The student teaches at their own level rather than the learner's. They demonstrate too fast, use vocabulary the learner does not know, or become frustrated when the learner makes mistakes. Safety supervision during teaching is inconsistent.

**Diagnostic questions:** After the teaching session, ask the student what they would do differently next time. A reflective answer indicates growth. A defensive answer ("the student just wasn't getting it") indicates the student is not yet ready for this level of responsibility.
